bytes.topic: Jetpack Compose vs View System — when to use each, interop
bytes.title: Jetpack Compose vs View System: When to Use Each for Interop
bytes.body: When integrating Compose with the traditional View system, use Compose for custom UI components and View for existing, non-Compose libraries. Compose's `remember` and `snapshotFlow` APIs facilitate data binding and UI updates. Use `View` when working with third-party libraries that don't have Compose equivalents.

bytes.topic: Kotlin Language — coroutines, flows, extension functions, data classes, sealed classes
bytes.title: Jetpack Compose — Optimizing UI Updates with LazyList and Coroutines
bytes.body: Compose's LazyList is often overlooked in favor of its simpler cousin, List. However, LazyList offers a significant performance boost when dealing with large datasets, especially when combined with coroutines. By leveraging coroutines to load data asynchronously, you can prevent UI freezes and improve user experience. To achieve this, use the LazyListState's load function to trigger data loading, and the onItemLoad callback to update the list state. This approach ensures seamless UI updates while minimizing unnecessary work.

bytes.topic: State Management — ViewModel, StateFlow, remember, rememberSaveable in Compose
bytes.title: jetpack_compose — Choosing the Right State Management Approach
bytes.body: When building UI components in jetpack_compose, you'll often encounter situations where you need to manage state. The framework offers several options for state management: ViewModel, StateFlow, remember, and rememberSaveable. ViewModel is ideal for complex business logic and data management. StateFlow is a more reactive approach suitable for real-time updates. remember and rememberSaveable are used for simple state management and persisting state between app restarts. To choose the right approach, consider the complexity of your state and the need for real-time updates.

bytes.topic: Networking — Retrofit, OkHttp, Kotlin serialization, error handling
bytes.title: Jetpack Compose — Efficiently Handling Network Errors with Retrofit
bytes.body: When using Retrofit with OkHttp in Jetpack Compose, you can handle network errors by creating a custom ExceptionHandler. This can be done by creating a class that implements the Retrofit2.CallAdapter.Factory interface. Then, in your Compose app, you can use the CoroutineScope to handle the network call and its potential errors. To demonstrate this, consider the following code snippet.

bytes.topic: Navigation — Navigation Component, Compose Navigation, deep links, back stack
bytes.title: Navigating Compose: Jetpack Compose Navigation Component
bytes.body: When working with Jetpack Compose, navigation is typically handled by the Navigation Component. This library provides a composable function, `NavController`, which can be used to create a back stack and navigate between destinations. Compose Navigation allows you to define navigation routes and destinations using the `NavGraph` API. To handle deep links, you can use the `DeepLink` API to parse the intent data and navigate to the corresponding destination. In Compose Navigation, you can use the `rememberNavController` function to create a `NavController` instance that persists across configuration changes.

bytes.topic: Dependency Injection — Hilt, Koin, why DI matters on Android
bytes.title: jetpack_compose: Mastering Dependency Injection with Hilt and Koin
bytes.body: When building complex jetpack_compose apps, managing dependencies between UI components becomes a challenge. Dependency Injection (DI) libraries like Hilt and Koin help decouple components, making it easier to test and maintain the app. Hilt, Google's official DI library, uses annotation processing to inject dependencies, while Koin relies on a more functional approach. To inject a dependency using Hilt, annotate the UI component with @Inject, and provide the dependency using @Provides. For example, to inject a data source into a composable function, use @Inject var dataSource: DataSource, and @Provides fun provideDataSource(): DataSource { return DataSourceImpl() }.

bytes.topic: Testing — JUnit, Mockk, Compose testing, Espresso for UI tests
bytes.title: Testing Compose UI with JUnit and Espresso
bytes.body: Compose UI components can be tested using JUnit with a UI test rule, which sets up a ComposeView and injects a CompositionContext. Use Mockk to mock dependencies. Espresso is used for UI tests, with Compose UI components rendered on a DisplayDevice. To test Compose UI, use the `composeRule` to create a `ComposeView` and `setContent` with your Compose UI component. You can then use Espresso to assert the UI's state.

Security — Android Keystore, network security config, ProGuard/R8
bytes.title: Secure Compose Apps with Jetpack Compose's Keystore and Network Security Config
bytes.body: When using Jetpack Compose, secure your app's sensitive data by storing it in the Android Keystore. Protect your compose components by using the compose resources' @DrawableRes and @StringRes annotations. For network security, add the <domain-name> tag to your app's AndroidManifest.xml file to restrict connections to specified domains.

bytes.topic: Material Design — Material3 in Compose, theming, dynamic color
bytes.title: Jetpack Compose — Dynamic Color with Material3 Theming
bytes.body: Material3 introduces a new way of theming in Jetpack Compose. To apply dynamic color to your app, you can use the `MaterialTheme` composable with the `colorScheme` parameter. This allows you to define a color scheme based on the device's theme, with options for light and dark modes. You can further customize the color scheme by creating a custom `ColorScheme` instance and passing it to the `MaterialTheme` composable.

bytes.topic: Push Notifications — FCM setup, notification channels, foreground vs background
bytes.title: jetpack_compose: Setting Up FCM Notifications with Channels
bytes.body: To integrate Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) with Jetpack Compose, you'll need to add the com.google.firebase:firebase-messaging dependency to your app's build.gradle file. Create a FirebaseMessagingService to handle incoming messages and a NotificationChannel to display notifications. When displaying notifications, use the LocalNotifications API to create a Notification. Ensure your app's notification channel is declared in the manifest.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — TalkBack, content descriptions, accessibility in Compose
Google Play — release tracks, in-app review, Play Feature Delivery
bytes.title: Jetpack Compose: Seamlessly Integrating Google Play Release Tracks, In-App Reviews, and Play Feature Delivery
bytes.body: When releasing Android apps, managing different versions and features can be complex. Jetpack Compose enables you to integrate Google Play release tracks, in-app reviews, and Play Feature Delivery with ease. To demonstrate, you can use the `androidx.compose.material3.MaterialTheme` to toggle between release tracks. For in-app reviews, the `androidx.compose.material3.Snackbar` can be used to prompt users to leave feedback. Meanwhile, Play Feature Delivery can be integrated using the `androidx.compose.runtime.CompositionLocal` to manage feature flags.

bytes.topic: Coroutines & Flows — structured concurrency, viewModelScope, collectAsState
App Architecture — MVVM, MVI, clean architecture with use cases
bytes.title: Choosing Between MVVM and MVI with Jetpack Compose
bytes.body: When it comes to structuring your Jetpack Compose app, two popular architectures are MVVM and MVI. MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) separates the app's logic into a ViewModel that exposes data to the UI, while MVI (Model-View-Intent) goes a step further by treating the app as a sequence of intents and responses. In Compose, you can use the `remember` and `mutableStateOf` APIs to implement both patterns. MVVM is particularly well-suited for Compose's declarative nature, as it allows you to easily update the UI when the ViewModel changes. On the other hand, MVI can provide a more predictable and testable architecture, but requires more boilerplate code.
